name: Alcoholic liver disease
description: Alcoholic liver disease — liver damage caused by chronic alcohol use, from fatty liver to alcoholic hepatitis and alcoholic cirrhosis.

en.name: Alcoholic liver disease
en.shortDescription: Alcoholic liver disease — liver damage caused by chronic alcohol use, from fatty liver to alcoholic hepatitis and alcoholic cirrhosis.
en.longDescription:
## Definition & Clinical Context

Alcoholic liver disease is the spectrum of liver damage caused by chronic alcohol use, ranging from reversible fatty liver (steatosis) through alcoholic hepatitis to alcoholic cirrhosis and alcoholic hepatic failure. In intensive care it is relevant as a comorbidity affecting coagulation, drug metabolism, infection risk and prognosis, and as a cause of admission (e.g. severe alcoholic hepatitis, decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis).

## Scope

The set is anchored on the SNOMED concept *201612 / 41309000 — Alcoholic liver damage* with all descendants included, so it captures the full spectrum: alcoholic fatty liver, alcoholic hepatitis (acute and chronic), alcoholic fibrosis, alcoholic cirrhosis, and alcoholic hepatic failure.

## Mapping Notes

- This set overlaps the Cirrhosis of liver set by a single concept (alcoholic cirrhosis): a patient with alcoholic cirrhosis maps to both sets, which is expected since the two variables answer different questions (alcohol as a cause vs. cirrhosis as an outcome).
- Non-alcoholic liver disease (e.g. viral hepatitis, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease) is **not** part of this set.
